- Fallbacks: automatic routing to lower-cost variants acceptable for non-critical queries, but trading endpoints must not fall back without explicit failover policy and customer consent.

Operational support / escalation:
- Support SLA: P1 (system down impacting trading) response < 15 minutes, P1 remediation updates every 30 minutes, 24/7 on-call; P2 < 60 minutes; P3 < 4 hours. Customer requests named on-call engineer for first 90 days post-go-live.
- Diagnostics: access to token-level latency traces, request logs (scrubbed PII), and billing breakdowns required for monthly review.

Security & compliance notes:
- CISO quote: "cannot accept key escrow — all keys must be customer-managed KMS; proof of key retention and rotation policy."
- Data residency: primary production in us-east-1, backups replicated to us-west-2; no eu data allowed for trading ML.

Commercials and GPU planning:
- Pricing sensitivity: willing to commit to 12-24 month reserved contract if SLA and maintenance windows acceptable. Estimated ARR band discussed $500k-$1.5M.
- GPU plan: initial reserved 8x A100 (80GB) per cluster; add 2 hot spares; option to burst to hosted pool (pay-as-you-go) for unpredictable spikes. Customer asked for clear lead time (4 weeks) for adding physical capacity in Dedicated.

Open legal redlines / blockers:
- Legal wants explicit language for scheduled maintenance caps and exclusion list; remove vague "reasonable efforts" language around credits.
- Need to clarify credit calculation baseline (is it regional availability zone or global cluster availability?).

Next steps (short):
- Redwood to provide SLA markups with explicit maintenance caps and sample credit clause by 2026-03-17.
- SE to deliver final GPU sizing quote and lead times; include hot-spare cost and burst rates.
- Legal to arrange redline session w/ Keystone legal (week of 2026-03-22).

Stubs / references: pricing deck and draft SLA in Drive (see linked_drive_docs). Fireflies IDs for conversation capture. Jira INFRA-1129 opened to capture provisioning lead-time question.

source.notes:
Summary: Enterprise procurement for Private VPC deployment. Heavy security focus (data residency EU option, KMS BYOK/HSM, audit log forwarding to customer SIEM).

Recent timeline:
- 2025-11-20: Initial intro call (Jordan). basic use-case: cockpit analytics + pilot support chat. asked about private deployment options.
- 2025-12-08: Demo of hosted API. positive on latency. asked for private VPC pricing.
- 2026-01-15: Architecture deep-dive with infra (Priya). discussed VPC endpoints, peering vs PrivateLink, egress control.
- 2026-02-18: Security intake call (Fireflies id above). procurement/infosec requested SIG/CAIQ and evidence for SOC2 controls.
- 2026-02-25: Uploaded preliminary SIG/CAIQ to procurement portal. Legal flagged gaps on key rotation & BYOK.
- 2026-03-05: Network review meeting; need CIDR, route table examples, NAT/bastion details.

Security questionnaire status:
- SIG/CAIQ: submitted (partial). security team requested encrypted evidence (pdfs) for 20+ control families.
- Vendor risk intake: Procurement opened intake ticket JIRA-INF-2110. assigned risk owner Sarah G.
- Shared responsibility: customer expects delineation for key management, audit retention, and VPC network controls. They want a formal matrix before final contract.

Technical requirements extracted:
- VPC-only private endpoints (no public egress by default).
- KMS integration: BYOK preferred; HSM-backed keys for model cache & logs. Key rotation policy required.
- Audit logs: full request/response metadata retained for 180 days in EU region; forward to customer SIEM (syslog/HTTP webhook).
- Data residency: primary region EU-West. fallbacks to NA only with prior approval.
- Authentication: SSO (SAML) + SCIM provisioning for RBAC.
- Compliance: Must provide SOC2 Type II report and ISO27001 certificate; pen-test summary for last 12 months.

POC & next milestones:
- Week of 2026-03-15: Security workshop with Sable Orbit infra + Redwood security (Priya + Mateo) — goal: finalize shared-resp matrix and KMS runbook.
- 2026-03-22: Deliver updated CAIQ with evidence zip and SOC2 attestation.
- 2026-03-29: Network diagram + example Terraform snippets for VPC peering and private endpoints.
- April 2026: Procurement review + legal SOW negotiation (target).

Quotes / customer color:
- "We can't have logs leave EU without explicit approval — that is a hard stop." — Sable Orbit InfoSec
- "Need BYOK, otherwise our security team will not sign." — Procurement lead

Open questions / items to own for Redwood:
- Provide documented shared-responsibility matrix (template from Security + annotated).
- KMS BYOK flow: key import vs HSM-backed rotation options; sample API callflow for key wraps.
- Audit log retention SLA and export capability (how to push to customer SIEM in near real-time).
- Network runbook: required ports, egress controls, example NAT configuration.

Action items for AE/SE/CSM:
- Jordan: coordinate procurement timeline and escalate SOC2 evidence to trust center.
- Priya (SE): prepare VPC Terraform snippets, KMS integration workshop slides.
- Mateo (CSM): draft onboarding checklist + 90-day rollout plan with canary routes.

Attachments: pricing deck (drive link), preliminary SIG/CAIQ export (drive link), Fireflies transcript link.

Short form next steps / blockers (CRM style):
- Next: Security workshop — confirm attendees + shared-resp matrix.
- Blocker: Legal needs full CAIQ + evidence zipped; procurement hold until SOC2 attestation verified.

CRM notes tone: lots of fragments, shorthand from calls, repeated asks from InfoSec. Keep tracking on procurement portal.

Observability ask: token-level cost breakdown per route + per-model slot visibility

notes: Primary contact: CTO (wants complete on-prem stack, no egress), SecOps lead is technical reviewer.

Latency SLO: 95th percentile reranked query <200ms for simple queries; tail target <500ms for long-context queries.
Cost sensitivity: aiming for <$0.03 per user query at current workload (heavy reuse via prefix + KV caching desirable).
Model preferences: embed with open checkpoints (instruction-tuned embed model), re-rank with small cross-encoder on dedicated infra; fallback path to quantized LLM if re-ranker unavailable.
Security: air-gapped install must include local KMS/HSM, no external telemetry by default, local-only telemetry opt-in for operations team.
Procurement notes: expects GSA/DoD contracting vehicle; will require SOW + 3rd party pen-test attestation before go-live.
Quote from SecOps: "We need binary-level assurance and a deterministic update window for the vector DB indexing process."
They requested a forensic logging sample and retention options (7+ years) — compliance is non-negotiable.
POC: 6-week pilot requested: ingest 5M docs, evaluate embed recall @ top-100, measure rerank latency and cost. POC success metrics defined below.
Notes from AE: prioritizing private/air-gapped variant; pricing will be a blended model (reserved capacity + support/engineering onboarding fee).

Throughput: baseline 250 requests/s across peak shard (50 RPS chat + 150 RPS embeddings batching + 50 RPS rerank), sustained token throughput target ~1.2M tokens/min during peak
Throughput guarantees requested: 99.9% of time at committed capacity, with documented graceful degradation beyond commit
Reserved GPU plan: capacity planning for 12–16 A100/MI300-class GPUs, support for mixed instance types for cost optimization
Burst capacity: ability to add 25% pool burst for 30-minute windows without manual approval

Credit formula: pro-rated service credits tied to region/cluster availability and degradation windows
Failover RTO objective: <120s for node/zone failure with automated routing; RPO target: zero for interactive chat (cache replication required)
Priority incident handoff: 24/7 escalation path + monthly reliability review
Performance regressions: requirement for pre-deploy performance runbooks and a rollback policy for model upgrades
reserved_gpu_plan: Primary purchase: 12 reserved A100 80GB (NA), immediate delivery quarter after contract
Regional redundancy: mirror 4 reserved GPUs in EU (hot standby) for failover and compliance
Autoscaling/burst: contract option for 4–6 burst GPUs billed at hourly uplift
Pricing ask: blended committed discount with explicit micrometrics for token execution cost per model variant
fallback_strategy: Model-variant fallback chain defined (70B->70B-q4->13B-q4->7B-distilled) with automatic CPU-level throttling when cluster reaches 85% GPU utilization
Graceful degradation: reduce max generation length and enable KV cache prefixing before switching model tier
Cross-region failover steps: 1) Enable DNS failover -> 2) Bring EU cluster out of standby -> 3) Rehydrate caches from snapshot + warm prefixes; expected additional latency during first 60s
Traffic shaping: redirect non-interactive batch jobs to lower-priority pools during failover windows
Monitoring: automated alerts on model switch, with immediate telemetry snapshot attached to incident ticket

source.notes:
Account summary: Aegis Cortex is a finserv platform offering real-time analytics for trading desks and fraud ops. Evaluating Redwood Dedicated for low-latency inference + private networking. Heavy emphasis on throughput guarantees for sustained 1k TPS across peak windows and 99th p50/p95 tail latency targets. Procurement & legal are the gating items.

Recent timeline:
- 2026-01-20: Intro call (AE Ava) - high-level product fit, AE demo, interested in Dedicated.
- 2026-02-10: Architecture deep-dive (SE Diego) - validated model types (mix of 7B/13B Llama variants + custom reranker), discussed autoscaling and reserved GPU pools.
- 2026-02-24: Capacity planning workshop - provided baseline token/sec per GPU numbers, proposed initial reservation: 8x A100-equivalent GPUs (reserved) + 2x burst.
- 2026-02-27: Workload profiling call (Fireflies ff_2026-02-27_4b9c3d) - customer sent sample logs, ran synthetic P99 tests; results: sustained throughput looks feasible but tail spikes on concurrent long contexts. Action: propose KV cache config + batching profile.
- 2026-03-03: Procurement + Security sync (Fireflies ff_2026-03-03_91f2a7) - legal provided DPA redlines, insurance broker calc, flagged SLA/penalty concerns.
- 2026-03-04: POC kickoff scheduled for 2026-03-10 (3-week POC). POC scope: validate throughput guarantees, 95/99 latency SLOs under mixed workload, autoscaling behavior, failover to hosted fallback.

POC/Technical requirements and acceptance criteria (customer quotes paraphrased):
- "We need consistent 1000 TPS with p95 < 50ms for embeddings and p99 < 200ms for chat completion under production mix."
- Throughput guarantee must include documented capacity planning and pre-warming steps.
- Prefer dedicated VPC deployment with customer-managed KMS.
- Want canary A/B rollout capability and on-demand burst for market open windows.
- Require audit logs retention 7 years for compliance team.

Procurement/security blockers (detailed):
- DPA/MSA redlines: Aegis legal insists on narrower indemnity, higher liability cap, and explicit SLAs tied to throughput (not just availability). They want an explicit remediation ladder for missed throughput SLOs (credits + right to terminate after X misses).
- SLA language: Current Redwood SLA model centers on uptime % and credit calculations. Customer insists on throughput-based credits (tokens/sec or request/sec windows) and clearer definition of what constitutes a breach (e.g., sustained 10% below committed throughput for 30m).
- Insurance: Broker requires minimum cyber/tech E&O limits that exceed standard Redwood template; procurement asks whether Redwood can provide proof of coverage and consider passing higher limits to insurance rider rather than contract amendment.
- Compliance add-ons: Need ISO27001 certificate and SOC2 Type II evidence; also data residency clauses for EU trade desks. These may trigger Private deployment add-on or specific routing rules and impact SLA (cross-region failover limitations).
- Contractual terms: Term length, termination for convenience penalties, and renewal pricing indexation need negotiation. Finance pushing for 12-month firm commit with quarterly usage reconciliation.
- Security posture: Customer requires pen-test reports and third-party vulnerability scans prior to go-live; wants remediation SLAs and a mechanism to escrow model weights if requested.

Sales strategy notes / playbook items:
- Align SE-led POC to prove throughput SLOs with real workloads early; measure both average and sustained windows (1m, 5m, 30m).
- Provide a dedicated SLA appendix draft that maps committed GPUs -> throughput targets -> credit ladder. Include pre-warm/scale-up timelines and acceptable blackout windows for maintenance.
- Loop legal early: propose a two-track negotiation - technical SLA appendix to sit alongside a narrower MSA/DPA to minimize redlines. Offer sample carrier insurance certificates and suggest using Redwood's recommended carrier + rider if needed.
- Offer a Private deployment pricing add-on to satisfy data residency and KMS requirements; clarify how this impacts escalation and SLA (e.g., single-region SLOs).

Next steps (action items):
- 2026-03-05: AE to upload draft SLA appendix to drive (drive:/deals/aegis-cortex/sla-appendix-draft-2026-03-05.docx).
- 2026-03-09: SE Diego to provision POC infra and run baseline synthetic throughput tests; share results by 2026-03-12.
- 2026-03-12: Legal to receive Redwood MSA + DPA redlines; internal legal will follow up with counter if needed.
- 2026-03-15: SLA negotiation call w/ procurement + legal + SE + AE (aim to resolve top-3 blockers).

Stitch notes / shorthand saved in CRM: procurement heavy, legal dance expected, technical feasibility confirmed but tail-latency mitigations necessary. If legal/insurance asks are within acceptable band we can close by 2026-05. Otherwise could slip to Q3.

Quoted lines from procurement: "we're fine paying for reserved capacity but need hard guarantees or an exit path if throughput claims are unmet".

Jira escalation: AI-3219 opened for POC infra flakiness (preliminary), OPS-7762 for quota/burst config.

Decision criteria (customer):
- Primary: documented throughput SLA mapped to reserved GPUs + measurable POC results.
- Secondary: contract liability/insurance acceptable to Aegis legal.
- Tertiary: integration with SSO/KMS and audit log retention policy.

Overall close probability: 60% (conditional on SLA appendix acceptance and manageable insurance terms).

notes: Summary: Mid-market fintech focused on customer-facing chat + document retrieval for payments dispute resolution.

Timeline: 2026-02-15 initial inbound via whitepaper download; AE intro call 2026-02-18; technical deep-dive with SE 2026-02-24; POC kickoff 2026-02-28; security review started 2026-03-01; final procurement conversations 2026-03-02.

POC: 2-week sandbox to validate chat latency (target <150ms tail for 512-token conv), embeddings for semantic search (monthly queries ~1.2M), and reranking for transaction anomaly classification. We provided a small test cluster on Dedicated trial config (4x A10G equiv) and suggested prefix caching to meet latency targets.
Security path: asked for SSO/SAML, audit logs, KMS integration. SE walked through Private options but customer said they'd prefer hosted due to speed of integration.
Decision drivers heard on final call: 'We value speed to market and brand familiarity. OpenAI is already in our infra playbooks and the dev team is comfortable with their SDKs.' — CTO paraphrase.
CTO comment: 'If it's easier to drop in OpenAI and ship in a couple weeks it's hard to justify a migration now. We can revisit multi-vendor routing later.'

Commercial: Redwood quoted estimated ARR $80k with 12-mo committed Dedicated discount. Procurement asked for alternate 'pay-as-you-go' pricing; we offered hosted tiers but they perceived direct OpenAI pricing as simpler and less overhead.

Key friction: no single executive to champion a longer integration; infra prefers first-party provider to minimize ops complexity; security team flagged KMS integration timeline as 'non-blocking but stretches resources'.

Competitor notes: POC also ran concurrently against OpenAI Chat/Embeddings and Anthropic Claude via direct APIs. Eval team reported faster dev iteration with OpenAI SDK and better sample responses for dispute resolution scenarios.
Activity log snippets:
- 2026-02-18 call (ff-20260218-4382): intro + product fit. AE notes: 'strong product fit but sensitive to integration overhead.'
- 2026-02-24 SE session: demo of KV cache and reranking. Action: deliver config snippets and cost estimates (done 2026-02-25).

- 2026-03-01 Security checklist submitted; requested timelines for KMS onboarding and audit log retention policies. Response provided 2026-03-03 but customer said timeline looked longer than internal target.

Loss reason (final): Customer selected OpenAI direct API for initial rollout due to developer familiarity, perceived lowest friction, and brand preference. Not a hard 'security fail' — more 'time-to-market' and 'no champion'.

Next actions taken: AE sent closing email 2026-03-04 with ROI snapshot + offer to stay on reserved queue. Follow-up reminder 2026-03-10 to ask for permission to keep POC artifacts; awaiting reply (unresponsive).

Internal learnings: need shorter KMS integration doc + ready-made migration playbook to counter 'brand + simplicity' wins. Consider one-click SDK migration samples from OpenAI payloads -> Redwood SDK.
Suggested contestability: offer limited-time 'migration Sprint' with SE-led integration to reduce perceived vendor switching cost. Capture for SDR nurture list.
Final status: Closed-lost (informal). Marking record for churn follow-up in 6 months.

Summary: Copperridge has been a hosted API customer since 2022 (billing under root account CR-AX). Renewal Q2 2026 — opportunity to upsell Dedicated (stable throughput for chat + reranking) and Redwood Optimize for 10-25% unit cost reduction seen during trial. Customer quote: "Need predictable p99 latency for peak bookings (<=120ms) and firm residency in eu-west for EU users."

Recent activity: short, choppy CRM-style notes below.
- 2026-02-04: AE intro + roadmap sync. Customer wants Dedicated sizing first; asked for 1000 concurrent chat tokens baseline.
- 2026-02-18: Deep dive w/SE (Rina) — shared Dedicated perf matrix and batching options; ran sample load test (see RDW-3251).
- 2026-02-25: Optimize pilot started (3 models, 2 weeks). Initial result: ~18% token cost reduction, 2% latency variance.
- 2026-03-01: Legal submitted data residency questionnaire; asked for SOC2 + data residency attestation and KMS options.
- 2026-03-02: Pricing call w/finance; customer pushed back on burst pricing tiers — requested fixed per-minute capacity model for 12-month commit.

POC/technical notes:
- POC: Canary in eu-west-1 using Dedicated pool with auto-burst to us-west for overflow. Latency target: p99 <=120ms for 4k context chat.
- Observability: wants token-level cost breakdown and per-route tracing in Console before commit.
- KMS: Must integrate with customer-managed AWS KMS; SE to validate key rotation flow.
- Model preferences: prefer open models compatible with quantized fp16/INT8; want fallback routing to smaller quantized variants under load.

Quotes from stakeholders:
- Head of ML Ops: "If we can get stable p99 and the KMS story, Dedicated is a no-brainer."
- Procurement: "Need legal + residency addendum before PO; prefer annual prepay."

Next steps (actionable):
1) SE to deliver final Dedicated sizing spreadsheet (Rina) by 2026-03-06.
2) Solutions doc & amended T&C (legal) — draft due 2026-03-10.
3) Final pricing call w/finance and procurement 2026-03-12 (owner: Ethan).
4) CSM to prep onboarding checklist for EU region (Marco).

source.notes:
Stage: procurement. Mid-market product analytics SaaS evaluating Hosted API first (POC in Dec) then Dedicated for predictable unit costs. Primary contact: CTO Marco Tan; Procurement lead: Elaine Park. Use-case: user-facing chat assistant (low-latency, 100-300 RPS spikes), embeddings for semantic search across product docs, reranking on search results.

Key technical/contract asks (from calls):
- Dedicated commit options: want two proposals: 1) committed GPU pool sized for 50k-100k tokens/day baseline OR 2) QPS-based commit (40 sustained r/s) with 3-month ramp.
- Burst: must support 2x baseline for 2 hours/day (expected during feature releases) — ask: is burst free within commit or capped?
- Overage: prefer predictable overage (not pay-as-you-go variable). Proposed Redwood draft: 20% overage cap at 1.5x unit price for first 6 months, then review.
- Ramp schedule: 3-month ramp to full commit; allow step increases month-over-month without penalty.
- Security: require VPC/private control plane + KMS integration, SOC2 evidence, SAML SSO + SCIM, audit logs retention 1 year, ability to set data residency region (us-east).

POC & evaluation notes:
- Dec 2025: hosted API POC (chat + embeddings) — internal metrics: p95 latency ~220ms on hosted endpoint with current prompt set; token cost higher than target.
- Jan 2026: SE deep-dive (Diego) ran stress test, identified batching/kv-cache opportunities; suggested quantized model variant for cost.
- Feb 17, 2026: Dedicated demo with infra team. Ran 10k-user simulated ramp; observed warm-up latency spike on cold KV cache.
- Feb 24, 2026: Pricing proposal v1 sent (commits in GPU-hours + burst pool add-on).
- Mar 1, 2026: Procurement call — legal flagged SLA credits language and overage tiering. Request to provide sample PO and redlines.

Quotes / customer voice: "Need predictable unit cost — our finance team won't accept token-level surprises." — Marco Tan (CTO).

Next steps (short):
- AE to send revised pricing (commit + capped overage + 3-month ramp schedule) by 2026-03-07.
- SE to produce a small ops runbook for KV warm-up and recommended batching/quantization knobs.
- Legal to exchange redlines on SLA by 2026-03-12.

Blockers: procurement approval window (procurement meets monthly), legal SLA markups, finalize burst policy and exact overage multiplier.

Linked artifacts: pricing deck (drive:/deals/aurora/pricing-deck-v2.pdf), security FAQ (drive:/security/aurora/security-faq.docx), call recordings (ff-2026-02-17-aurora-demo).

source.notes:
CRM notes:
- Account: Meridian Pulse Works (mid-market SaaS, product analytics).
- Primary contact: Riley Park (VP Product), engages with infra lead Samir J. and finance mgr Nora L.
- Use-case: customer-facing chat assistant for product docs + vector search for support workflows. Also embeddings for similarity and summarization for feature notes.
- Performance targets: target p95 latency ~100-150ms for chat; throughput ~40 rps avg, peak test saw 120 rps. Cost sensitive — finance wants predictable unit economics.
- POC summary: hosted API POC deemed functionally successful. Observed p95 tail when concurrent burst simulated; SE suspects cache warming + KV eviction under load.
- Model preference: open-family models OK (Redwood-verified profiles preferred). Must be able to pin model version and fallback to smaller variant under capacity pressure.
- Security / deployment: require VPC peering or private endpoint, SOC2 attestation, KMS integration, US-only data residency for now. SSO/SAML integration mandatory.
- Pricing asks from Meridian: 12- and 24-month commit options; monthly smoothing (commit ramp); burst pool at 2x baseline; hard overage cap or step-pricing rather than open per-token overage. Finance asked for a sample ARR schedule showing ramp Q2->Q4.
- Redwood offer at last draft: three commit bands (250k, 500k, 1M token-equivalent per month), 2x burst credits, overage $0.006/token for smallest band, sliding discount for higher commits. Proposed 3-month ramp where first month billed at 50% commit.
- Meridian feedback: likes burst model but wants explicit overage ceiling and invoice predictability. Legal pushed back on tail-latency SLA language — needs clearer measurement and remedy.
- Quotes: Riley: "We need to lock ARR visibility for the year — can’t have surprise overages."
- Internal notes: SE Diego recommends enabling prefix caching and conservative batch sizing on Dedicated; Ops suggests reserved pool w/ autoscale + pre-warm to reduce tail-latency.
- Risks: procurement timeline (license committee) could push deal to 2026-05. Technical risk: peak tail-latency without KV cache tuning.
- Wins: team liked Redwood Console traces and cost breakdown; hosted POC showed 25% cost improvement vs current infra when batching and cache enabled.
- Next step: AE to update term sheet with a tightened overage cap, explicit ramp schedule, and example invoice; schedule procurement review.

source.notes:
Background: mid-market B2B SaaS (product analytics + in-app coach). Started on Redwood hosted API (Jan 2026 trial), now moving toward Dedicated for consistent tail latency and data isolation.

Engagement history (high-level):
- 2026-01-12: initial discovery call (Jordan) — product wants in-app chat and document search; latency target p95 < 300ms for chat.
- 2026-01-20: hosted API PoC onboarded, 2-week trial; integrated chat SDK + embeddings. Saw ~15% cost saving vs OAI baseline (early).
- 2026-02-08: architecture sync with SE (Maya) — discussed KV cache, routing to smaller quantized models for low-cost requests.
- 2026-02-18: feature demo for execs (Fireflies ff_2026-02-18_0342) — execs asked for SLA + VPC for customer data.
- 2026-02-25: internal benchmark run (hosted) — throughput ~80 reqs/s with burst spikes; tail latency occasionally hits 420ms under load.
- 2026-03-01: Dedicated pricing review (Jordan + Maya + finance) — shared committed GPU-hours profile and draft ramp schedule (drive:/decks/silverdelta/redwood-pricing-draft-v3.pdf). Fireflies ff_2026-03-01_1129 noted concerns re: overage rates.

Use-case / requirements summary:
- Primary: in-app conversational assistant (chat) + semantic doc search for onboarding flows. Secondary: reranking for personalized recommendations.
- Latency: target p95 chat < 300ms; avg embedding latency < 120ms.
- Throughput: expected steady-state 50-120 qps per region during business hours; spikes to ~300 qps for weekly digest batch processing.
- Cost sensitivity: mid-high; must justify Dedicated commit vs hosted unit price. Expecting 12–18 month ROI timeline.
- Model preferences: open models (Llama 2 variants, possible Realtime quantized variants). Want Redwood-verified perf profiles for selected model family.
- Routing/fallback: require automatic fallback to smaller quantized variant when Dedicated capacity constrained; cross-region fallback acceptable with data residency controls.

Pricing / procurement specifics discussed so far:
- Proposal from Redwood: 3-tier commit proposal: (a) 6-month pilot commit (reserve 40% of expected baseline), (b) 12-month standard commit (comfort zone), (c) 24-month discount commit with steeper ramp.
- Key negotiation points: guaranteed baseline vs flexible burst credits, overage pricing (flat per-GPU-hour vs per-token), ramp schedule (month-by-month vs front-loaded credits), and termination/true-up clauses.
- SilverDelta asks: ability to bank unused burst credits for 90 days; predictable monthly invoice with usage reconciliation; option to double burst capacity for 48h window at pre-negotiated premium.
- Finance comment (email thread-pricing-67890): prefer blended rate cap rather than complex tiered overage.

Security / compliance notes:
- SSO: will require SAML integration for platform access.
- Data residency: primary customers in NA/EU — want EU region support for embeddings and retention controls.
- Legal: information security questionnaire in progress; security doc answers uploaded to drive:/security/silverdelta-security-answers.xlsx.

POC / technical checklist still open:
- Finalize network topology for VPC peering and egress rules.
- Confirm KMS workflow for model keys and audit log retention duration.
- Run dedicated pilot on 2x A10 GPUs for 2 weeks with production traffic shadowing, measure p95 and cost per 1k tokens.

Quotes / customer sentiment snippets:
- "We need predictable unit economics for FY27 budgeting — surprises kill renewals." — Head of Product.
- "If Dedicated cuts p95 by 20% and gives predictable pricing, it's a no-brainer." — CTO (paraphrase).

Next steps (short):
- Jordan to circulate revised term sheet by 2026-03-10 with three commit options and explicit burst/overage examples.
- Maya to provision dedicated pilot environment (VPC + 2 GPUs) for shadow traffic by 2026-03-15.
- Legal to receive SOW and draft PO template; finance to approve blended rate model or accept cap by 2026-03-22.

Sales notes / tactics:
- Emphasize Redwood Optimize recommendations that show expected cost delta with batching/kv caching; present modeled run-rate with/without quantization.
- Offer short-term promotional burst credits to lower pilot friction (one-time).
- Prepare comparison sheet vs Anthropic/OpenAI for tail latency and committed pricing.

source.notes:
Status: closed-lost (decisioned 2026-03-02). Lost to incumbent (OpenAI) on quality for structured output and tool-calling reliability.

Summary of engagement:
- 2025-11-18: Intro call (AE: Ethan Park) — Helix interested in migrating a reranking + chat layer from in-house GPT-based setup to Redwood for cost predictability and observability. Security org insisted on VPC or on-prem.
- 2025-12-02: Demo: Redwood hosted API + Optimize suggestions. Good fit on cost controls, AE ran token-level cost scenarios. SE (Maya) demoed model-routing and fallback.
- 2026-01-10: POC kickoff (2-week). Goal: match incumbent's structured JSON outputs for compliance summaries and enable reliable function/tool calls for an external risk-scoring service. Provided sample prompt set and eval metrics.
- 2026-01-20: Security questionnaire submitted, asked for on-prem deployment checklist and KMS/HSM diagrams. Security team asked for SOC2 + ISO guidance.
- 2026-01-28: Mid-POC check-in. Early red flags: Redwood model variant produced inconsistent key ordering and occasional malformed JSON; function-calling semantics sometimes omitted required fields. Logging/tracing looked good but outputs failed JSON schema validation at 12% higher rate vs incumbent.
- 2026-02-14: Deep dive with product + SEs. We tested deterministic function-calling path and structured-output constraints. SEs implemented stricter schema enforcement in prompt + post-processing suggestion, but Helix ran blind evals and reported lower precision on entity extraction compared to incumbent.
- 2026-02-21: Helix legal and security escalate — ask for on-prem timeline and SLA for private control plane. They also asked for an explicit remediation plan for model quality regressions (metric baselines, rollback policy).
- 2026-02-25: Internal budget freeze at Helix due to re-org; champion (Director of AI Platforms) put purchase on hold, then left the company (internal move) 2026-02-28. No clear replacement.
- 2026-03-02: Final decision: choose incumbent (OpenAI) due to superior out-of-the-box structured output and more mature function-calling guarantees. Also accepted that incumbent runs in their preferred VPC configuration already.

Key technical learnings / flagged items:
- Structured-output reliability was the primary differentiator. Redwood required prompt + Optimize tuning; incumbent achieved lower schema fail rate without extra engineering.
- Function/tool calling: Helix emphasized mandatory required-field guarantees and deterministic invocation ordering. Redwood's runtime had non-deterministic edge cases in the POC prompts we gave.
- Observability: Helix liked Redwood Console token-level costs and tracing — seen as a net positive but not decisive.
- Security: Helix needed on-prem option with KMS integration and SOC2 evidence — we provided docs but timeline was too long for them.

AE notes / next actions for archive:
- Capture precise failing prompt examples in repo (done, linked).
- Feed POC eval metrics back to model onboarding team and Optimize roadmap: work item to improve structured-output guidance + more robust function-calling mode.
- Re-engagement plan: target Q4 2026 after Helix stabilizes budget and hires new AI platform lead. Offer a short follow-up audit (free) focused on structured-output parity.

Customer quote snippets captured: "Output looks close but we keep getting broken JSON for edge-case invoices", "Function calls are missing fields intermittently which breaks our scoring pipeline".

Attachments: POC results PDF, security questionnaire, sample failing prompts saved in drive.

source.notes:
Summary: Large EU-headquartered manufacturing automation vendor evaluating Redwood Private (VPC) for on-prem-like isolation. Primary drivers: data residency in EU, HSM-backed key control, demonstrable audit trails, and strict network isolation. They ran a 3-week POC (chat + document search) on Dedicated; now moving to procurement for private VPC.

Security intake / vendor risk: Legal & InfoSec requested full SIG/CAIQ deliverable. They want:
- Completed SIG questionnaire with evidence links (SOC2 cert, encryption diagrams).
- CAIQ mappings to our control implementations and a shared-responsibility table that their security team can drop into their vendor-risk system.
- Explicit network architecture: VPC peering vs. PrivateLink, NAT/egress controls, firewall rules, allowed IP ranges, and flow logs retention policy.

KMS / key management:
- Customer requires customer-managed keys (CMK) with HSM-backed KMS. Must show how Redwood integrates KMS (bring-your-own-KMS) and what keys we hold (if any).
- Need an HSM/PKI diagram and a statement of where keys are generated, rotated, and stored.
- Clarify emergency access procedures and key escrow options.

Audit logging & retention:
- Ask: retention windows (default 90 days), export formats (parquet/json), and who has access.
- They want audit logs pushed to their S3/GCS in EU region and ability to revoke Redwood console access while preserving logs.

Data residency & residency proof:
- All inference and embeddings must remain in EU region. They require attestations and a data-flow diagram showing no tokenized copy leaves region.

POC feedback (technical):
- Latency SLOs met for single-region workload (avg p95 ~120ms for chat with quantized model).
- Token costs OK but ops want Optimize suggestions to reduce tail tokens.
- Need latency comparison between dedicated pool and private VPC due to additional network hop.

Sales / commercial:
- Favoring 12–18 month committed capacity contract (Dedicated + Private overlay).
- Procurement wants license + platform fee separated; need final SOW and MSA redlines.

Quotes/paraphrases: "We need provable control of keys and logs — if you can't give us the KMS model, we can't go forward."

Immediate asks from Lyra:
1) Fill and return SIG + CAIQ spreadsheet (security team deadline 2026-03-05).
2) Provide VPC architecture diagram showing private endpoints and log export flows (network deadline 2026-03-01).
3) Draft shared-responsibility matrix (sample provided).

Internal notes: SE Diego to produce KMS/HSM diagram + sign-off Mgr. AE Ava to coordinate legal redlines with Sarah (legal). CSM Priya to prepare onboarding checklist for post-sign.

CRM shorthand:
timeline: 2025-10-01 - Lead created after referral from channel partner (EU systems integrator)
2025-11-03 - POC kickoff (dedicated pool): chat + doc-search workloads; SE Diego led setup
2025-11-24 - POC demo; latency metrics shared (p95 ~120ms) + cost baseline
2025-12-10 - Security intake: initial SIG request; Lyra provided vendor-risk template
2026-01-12 - Deep dive: KMS requirements call (Fireflies ff_2026-01-12_lyra-security-review)
2026-02-05 - Commercial: pricing sketch and committed capacity options sent
2026-02-20 - Legal: MSA redlines submitted by Lyra; AE responded with counter-terms
2026-02-28 - Procurement sync: confirm timeline for SIG/CAIQ return and SOW updates
